<title>Comparative life cycle assessment for conventional and organic coffee cultivation in Vietnam </title>
L.T.K. Trinh1 A. H. Hul Y. Lan2 Z.H. Chen2
Received: 17 December 2018 / Revised: 12 March 2019 / Accepted: 14 September 2019 / Published online: 23 September 2019 © Islamic Azad University (IAU) 2019
1: Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

Many LCA papers found no variation between perennial and annual crops because the inventory data of these studies are based on only 1-year collection of available information (Bessou et al. 2012). Moreover, usually limited explanations of the collected information were provided regarding the stage of coffee's lifespan and existence of yield difference in the vari- ous levels (Bessou et al. 2012). Therefore, the novelty of this research work is the use of a longitudinal study with the inventory data collected and analyzed throughout 30 years of coffee lifespan. A summary of LCAs in coffee is shown in Table 1. Noponen et al. (2012) performed an in-depth study on coffee cultivation. Their results showed that carbon footprint (CF) for 1 kg of coffee cherries was 0.26-0.67 and 0.12-0.52 kg CO2e for conventional and organic systems, respectively. The inputs of organic and inorganic nitrogen are the main contributors to greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions for all management systems. Nitrous oxide emissions from pruning inputs contributed between 7 and 42% of CFs (Noponen et al. 2012). Killian et al. (2013) conducted com- prehensive study about CF across the coffee supply chain: The case of Costa Rican coffee has determined the CF of the supply chain of Costa Rican, which is exported to European countries. According to the results, the total CF across the entire supply chain is 4.98 kg CO2e per 1 kg of dry coffee bean. The CF of the processes in Costa Rica for 1 kg of green coffee as FU is 1.02 kg CO2e at the farm level. The sources of the highest contribution to environmental impact are also discussed in the study (Killian et al. 2013).

The below-mentioned list shows that LCA research on coffee is highly diversified, especially in some highly pro- ducing and consuming countries, such as Brazil and Costa Rica. Intensive research on the environmental effect of coffee in Vietnam is insufficient despite being the second largest coffee producer in the world after Brazil and top producer of Robusta coffee (BBC News 2014; FAO 2017). As described in the table, only one study by Humbert et al. (2009) conducted the evaluation of coffee in Vietnam, but this work focuses on coffee as a drink with a FU of 1 dL cup coffee. Substantial research confirmed that the cultiva- tion stage occupies a high proportion of the tram in causing environmental effects (Noponen et al. 2012; Coltro et al. 2006; Salomone 2003). Therefore, an in-depth study of the coffee cultivation stage in Vietnam is needed to clarify the environmental impacts.

The weather and climate in Vietnam are highly suit- able for growing coffee given that the country is located in the tropical belt of the Northern Hemisphere (Amaras- inghe et al. 2015). Coffee species in the world are classi- fied into two types (i.e., Robusta and Arabica). More than 90% of Robusta coffee trees in Vietnam are found in the Tay Nguyen area owing to the country's climate condi- tion. Due to climatic conditions, Vietnam can only produce

Robusta which accounts for 90%. Robusta is short name of Coffea canephora Robusta, which is popular worldwide and has long been used for producing instant coffee. Grow- ing Robusta requires several conditions, such as excellent soil, suitable weather and high level of cultivation with substantial input of water, fertilizer and manure. Moreover, Robusta exhibits not only high productivity but also great flavor. According to the Ministry of Industry and Trade, cof- fee exports of Vietnam were 1.269 million tons from Octo- ber 2014 to September 2015, thereby bringing in more than $2.648 billion and increasing the value of their shipments in 2016 to $3.2 billion (GSO 2016). The coffee growing area and productivity in Vietnam have rapidly expanded from 13,000 ha with a total yield of 6000 tons in 1975 to 605,000 ha with a total yield of 1.65 million tons in 2017; most of these areas are in Tay Nguyen (GAIN report 2017). Agricultural cultivation is highly important to the preserva- tion and improvement in the quality of natural condition and thus requires careful management. Furthermore, unsus- tainable agricultural activities such as intensive monocul- ture greatly affect the environment, water quality, soil, air and human health. Therefore, research on coffee cultiva- tion should be intensively studied. However, no study that shows the negative impact of conventional coffee cultivation in Vietnam on the environment and human health is cur- rently available.

Currently, there are three main coffee cultivation meth- ods that are classified based on input level which are: con- ventional intensive (CI), conventional moderate (CM) and organic intensive (OI) (Noponen et al. 2012). Conventional cultivation produces some of the most heavily chemically treated foods in the world with the high accumulation of synthetic fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides (Pimentel et al. 2005). In Vietnam, CI is the most popu- lar cultivation method due to the high yields and economic benefits. However, this method will have significant nega- tive effects to the environment and human health (Noponen et al. 2012). The CI is a coffee cultivation method that uses considerable amounts of chemical fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides. The CM method in this study is defined as conventional farm without pesticides. Therefore, its negative effects on the environment are much less than those of CI. However, there are no synthetic fertilizers or chemicals, which are used in organic cultivation, thereby indicating high-quality coffee beans and great land, water and air quality where coffee is grown using organic manures, such as coffee pulp, animal manure or compost. Organic farms have moderate effects to the environment given their low-carbon emission. Thus, organic coffee beans are rich in antioxidants and compounds that benefit human health. Many LCA papers found no variation between perennial and annual crops because the inventory data of these studies are based on only 1-year collection of available information (Bessou et al. 2012). Moreover, usually limited explanations of the collected information were provided regarding the stage of coffee's lifespan and existence of yield difference in the vari- ous levels (Bessou et al. 2012). Therefore, the novelty of this research work is the use of a longitudinal study with the inventory data collected and analyzed throughout 30 years of coffee lifespan. A summary of LCAs in coffee is shown in Table 1. Noponen et al. (2012) performed an in-depth study on coffee cultivation. Their results showed that carbon footprint (CF) for 1 kg of coffee cherries was 0.26-0.67 and 0.12-0.52 kg CO2e for conventional and organic systems, respectively. The inputs of organic and inorganic nitrogen are the main contributors to greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions for all management systems. Nitrous oxide emissions from pruning inputs contributed between 7 and 42% of CFs (Noponen et al. 2012). Killian et al. (2013) conducted com- prehensive study about CF across the coffee supply chain: The case of Costa Rican coffee has determined the CF of the supply chain of Costa Rican, which is exported to European countries. According to the results, the total CF across the entire supply chain is 4.98 kg CO2e per 1 kg of dry coffee bean. The CF of the processes in Costa Rica for 1 kg of green coffee as FU is 1.02 kg CO2e at the farm level. The sources of the highest contribution to environmental impact are also discussed in the study (Killian et al. 2013).

The below-mentioned list shows that LCA research on coffee is highly diversified, especially in some highly pro- ducing and consuming countries, such as Brazil and Costa Rica. Intensive research on the environmental effect of coffee in Vietnam is insufficient despite being the second largest coffee producer in the world after Brazil and top producer of Robusta coffee (BBC News 2014; FAO 2017). As described in the table, only one study by Humbert et al. (2009) conducted the evaluation of coffee in Vietnam, but this work focuses on coffee as a drink with a FU of 1 dL cup coffee. Substantial research confirmed that the cultiva- tion stage occupies a high proportion of the tram in causing environmental effects (Noponen et al. 2012; Coltro et al. 2006; Salomone 2003). Therefore, an in-depth study of the coffee cultivation stage in Vietnam is needed to clarify the environmental impacts.

The weather and climate in Vietnam are highly suit- able for growing coffee given that the country is located in the tropical belt of the Northern Hemisphere (Amaras- inghe et al. 2015). Coffee species in the world are classi- fied into two types (i.e., Robusta and Arabica). More than 90% of Robusta coffee trees in Vietnam are found in the Tay Nguyen area owing to the country's climate condi- tion. Due to climatic conditions, Vietnam can only produce

Robusta which accounts for 90%. Robusta is short name of Coffea canephora Robusta, which is popular worldwide and has long been used for producing instant coffee. Grow- ing Robusta requires several conditions, such as excellent soil, suitable weather and high level of cultivation with substantial input of water, fertilizer and manure. Moreover, Robusta exhibits not only high productivity but also great flavor. According to the Ministry of Industry and Trade, cof- fee exports of Vietnam were 1.269 million tons from Octo- ber 2014 to September 2015, thereby bringing in more than $2.648 billion and increasing the value of their shipments in 2016 to $3.2 billion (GSO 2016). The coffee growing area and productivity in Vietnam have rapidly expanded from 13,000 ha with a total yield of 6000 tons in 1975 to 605,000 ha with a total yield of 1.65 million tons in 2017; most of these areas are in Tay Nguyen (GAIN report 2017). Agricultural cultivation is highly important to the preserva- tion and improvement in the quality of natural condition and thus requires careful management. Furthermore, unsus- tainable agricultural activities such as intensive monocul- ture greatly affect the environment, water quality, soil, air and human health. Therefore, research on coffee cultiva- tion should be intensively studied. However, no study that shows the negative impact of conventional coffee cultivation in Vietnam on the environment and human health is cur- rently available.

Currently, there are three main coffee cultivation meth- ods that are classified based on input level which are: con- ventional intensive (CI), conventional moderate (CM) and organic intensive (OI) (Noponen et al. 2012). Conventional cultivation produces some of the most heavily chemically treated foods in the world with the high accumulation of synthetic fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides (Pimentel et al. 2005). In Vietnam, CI is the most popu- lar cultivation method due to the high yields and economic benefits. However, this method will have significant nega- tive effects to the environment and human health (Noponen et al. 2012). The CI is a coffee cultivation method that uses considerable amounts of chemical fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides. The CM method in this study is defined as conventional farm without pesticides. Therefore, its negative effects on the environment are much less than those of CI. However, there are no synthetic fertilizers or chemicals, which are used in organic cultivation, thereby indicating high-quality coffee beans and great land, water and air quality where coffee is grown using organic manures, such as coffee pulp, animal manure or compost. Organic farms have moderate effects to the environment given their low-carbon emission. Thus, organic coffee beans are rich in antioxidants and compounds that benefit human health. Materials and methods
4: <header>Abstract </header>

Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

Grow- ing Robusta requires several conditions, such as excellent soil, suitable weather and high level of cultivation with substantial input of water, fertilizer and manure. Moreover, Robusta exhibits not only high productivity but also great flavor. Materials and methods
6: <header>Abstract </header>

Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

Materials and methods
8: Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

10: <header>Abstract </header>

Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

Many LCA papers found no variation between perennial and annual crops because the inventory data of these studies are based on only 1-year collection of available information (Bessou et al. 2012). Moreover, usually limited explanations of the collected information were provided regarding the stage of coffee's lifespan and existence of yield difference in the vari- ous levels (Bessou et al. 2012). Therefore, the novelty of this research work is the use of a longitudinal study with the inventory data collected and analyzed throughout 30 years of coffee lifespan. A summary of LCAs in coffee is shown in Table 1. Noponen et al. (2012) performed an in-depth study on coffee cultivation. Their results showed that carbon footprint (CF) for 1 kg of coffee cherries was 0.26-0.67 and 0.12-0.52 kg CO2e for conventional and organic systems, respectively. The inputs of organic and inorganic nitrogen are the main contributors to greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions for all management systems. Nitrous oxide emissions from pruning inputs contributed between 7 and 42% of CFs (Noponen et al. 2012). Killian et al. (2013) conducted com- prehensive study about CF across the coffee supply chain: The case of Costa Rican coffee has determined the CF of the supply chain of Costa Rican, which is exported to European countries. According to the results, the total CF across the entire supply chain is 4.98 kg CO2e per 1 kg of dry coffee bean. The CF of the processes in Costa Rica for 1 kg of green coffee as FU is 1.02 kg CO2e at the farm level. The sources of the highest contribution to environmental impact are also discussed in the study (Killian et al. 2013).

The below-mentioned list shows that LCA research on coffee is highly diversified, especially in some highly pro- ducing and consuming countries, such as Brazil and Costa Rica. Intensive research on the environmental effect of coffee in Vietnam is insufficient despite being the second largest coffee producer in the world after Brazil and top producer of Robusta coffee (BBC News 2014; FAO 2017). As described in the table, only one study by Humbert et al. (2009) conducted the evaluation of coffee in Vietnam, but this work focuses on coffee as a drink with a FU of 1 dL cup coffee. Substantial research confirmed that the cultiva- tion stage occupies a high proportion of the tram in causing environmental effects (Noponen et al. 2012; Coltro et al. 2006; Salomone 2003). Therefore, an in-depth study of the coffee cultivation stage in Vietnam is needed to clarify the environmental impacts.

The weather and climate in Vietnam are highly suit- able for growing coffee given that the country is located in the tropical belt of the Northern Hemisphere (Amaras- inghe et al. 2015). Coffee species in the world are classi- fied into two types (i.e., Robusta and Arabica). More than 90% of Robusta coffee trees in Vietnam are found in the Tay Nguyen area owing to the country's climate condi- tion. Due to climatic conditions, Vietnam can only produce

Robusta which accounts for 90%. Robusta is short name of Coffea canephora Robusta, which is popular worldwide and has long been used for producing instant coffee. Grow- ing Robusta requires several conditions, such as excellent soil, suitable weather and high level of cultivation with substantial input of water, fertilizer and manure. Moreover, Robusta exhibits not only high productivity but also great flavor. According to the Ministry of Industry and Trade, cof- fee exports of Vietnam were 1.269 million tons from Octo- ber 2014 to September 2015, thereby bringing in more than $2.648 billion and increasing the value of their shipments in 2016 to $3.2 billion (GSO 2016). The coffee growing area and productivity in Vietnam have rapidly expanded from 13,000 ha with a total yield of 6000 tons in 1975 to 605,000 ha with a total yield of 1.65 million tons in 2017; most of these areas are in Tay Nguyen (GAIN report 2017). Agricultural cultivation is highly important to the preserva- tion and improvement in the quality of natural condition and thus requires careful management. Furthermore, unsus- tainable agricultural activities such as intensive monocul- ture greatly affect the environment, water quality, soil, air and human health. Therefore, research on coffee cultiva- tion should be intensively studied. However, no study that shows the negative impact of conventional coffee cultivation in Vietnam on the environment and human health is cur- rently available.

Currently, there are three main coffee cultivation meth- ods that are classified based on input level which are: con- ventional intensive (CI), conventional moderate (CM) and organic intensive (OI) (Noponen et al. 2012). Conventional cultivation produces some of the most heavily chemically treated foods in the world with the high accumulation of synthetic fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides (Pimentel et al. 2005). In Vietnam, CI is the most popu- lar cultivation method due to the high yields and economic benefits. However, this method will have significant nega- tive effects to the environment and human health (Noponen et al. 2012). Organic farms have moderate effects to the environment given their low-carbon emission. Thus, organic coffee beans are rich in antioxidants and compounds that benefit human health. 12: <header>Abstract </header>

Coffee production has been a major source of income in Vietnam since the early twentieth century. This research aims to identify the hot spots, estimate and compare the environmental effects of conventional intensive, conventional moderate and organic intensive coffee cultivation methods in Vietnam. Life cycle assessment was used for the determination of environ- mental effects and carbon footprint for different coffee cultivation methods from cradle to gate. Functional unit was defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District, Lam Dong Province. The environmental effects of coffee cultivation were compared by SimaPro 8.3.0, and the two impact assessment methods used were IPCC 2013 v1.03 and ReCiPe v1.13. The life cycle assessment results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesticide application showed that the conventional intensive contributed 85.5% to global warming owing to the high input of manure, whereas conventional moderate and organic intensive contributed 80.4% and 68% to global warming, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, endpoint impact result indicated that human health is most affected by coffee cultivation compared to resources and ecosystem. The carbon footprint result of 1-year average productivity showed that the conventional intensive (0.935 kg CO2e) method had the highest global warming potential in comparison with conventional moderate (0.729 kg COe) and organic intensive (0.644 kg CO2e) due to the highest amount of fertilizer application. This study demonstrated that conventional intensive has the highest impact on the environment, followed by conventional moderate and organic intensive. Therefore, it is important to optimize Vietnamese coffee cultivation methods in order to reduce the impact on the environment and human health, while producing sustainable coffee for the international and domestic market.
Keywords Environmental impact . Carbon footprint . Coffea canephora Robusta . Global warming potential . Human health . Sustainable coffee cultivation

Many LCA papers found no variation between perennial and annual crops because the inventory data of these studies are based on only 1-year collection of available information (Bessou et al. 2012). A summary of LCAs in coffee is shown in Table 1. Noponen et al. (2012) performed an in-depth study on coffee cultivation. Their results showed that carbon footprint (CF) for 1 kg of coffee cherries was 0.26-0.67 and 0.12-0.52 kg CO2e for conventional and organic systems, respectively. The inputs of organic and inorganic nitrogen are the main contributors to greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions for all management systems. Nitrous oxide emissions from pruning inputs contributed between 7 and 42% of CFs (Noponen et al. 2012). Killian et al. (2013) conducted com- prehensive study about CF across the coffee supply chain: The case of Costa Rican coffee has determined the CF of the supply chain of Costa Rican, which is exported to European countries. According to the results, the total CF across the entire supply chain is 4.98 kg CO2e per 1 kg of dry coffee bean. The CF of the processes in Costa Rica for 1 kg of green coffee as FU is 1.02 kg CO2e at the farm level. The sources of the highest contribution to environmental impact are also discussed in the study (Killian et al. 2013).

The below-mentioned list shows that LCA research on coffee is highly diversified, especially in some highly pro- ducing and consuming countries, such as Brazil and Costa Rica. Intensive research on the environmental effect of coffee in Vietnam is insufficient despite being the second largest coffee producer in the world after Brazil and top producer of Robusta coffee (BBC News 2014; FAO 2017). As described in the table, only one study by Humbert et al. (2009) conducted the evaluation of coffee in Vietnam, but this work focuses on coffee as a drink with a FU of 1 dL cup coffee. Substantial research confirmed that the cultiva- tion stage occupies a high proportion of the tram in causing environmental effects (Noponen et al. 2012; Coltro et al. 2006; Salomone 2003). Therefore, an in-depth study of the coffee cultivation stage in Vietnam is needed to clarify the environmental impacts.

The weather and climate in Vietnam are highly suit- able for growing coffee given that the country is located in the tropical belt of the Northern Hemisphere (Amaras- inghe et al. 2015). Coffee species in the world are classi- fied into two types (i.e., Robusta and Arabica). More than 90% of Robusta coffee trees in Vietnam are found in the Tay Nguyen area owing to the country's climate condi- tion. Due to climatic conditions, Vietnam can only produce

Robusta which accounts for 90%. Robusta is short name of Coffea canephora Robusta, which is popular worldwide and has long been used for producing instant coffee. Grow- ing Robusta requires several conditions, such as excellent soil, suitable weather and high level of cultivation with substantial input of water, fertilizer and manure. Moreover, Robusta exhibits not only high productivity but also great flavor. According to the Ministry of Industry and Trade, cof- fee exports of Vietnam were 1.269 million tons from Octo- ber 2014 to September 2015, thereby bringing in more than $2.648 billion and increasing the value of their shipments in 2016 to $3.2 billion (GSO 2016). The coffee growing area and productivity in Vietnam have rapidly expanded from 13,000 ha with a total yield of 6000 tons in 1975 to 605,000 ha with a total yield of 1.65 million tons in 2017; most of these areas are in Tay Nguyen (GAIN report 2017). Agricultural cultivation is highly important to the preserva- tion and improvement in the quality of natural condition and thus requires careful management. Furthermore, unsus- tainable agricultural activities such as intensive monocul- ture greatly affect the environment, water quality, soil, air and human health. Therefore, research on coffee cultiva- tion should be intensively studied. However, no study that shows the negative impact of conventional coffee cultivation in Vietnam on the environment and human health is cur- rently available.

Currently, there are three main coffee cultivation meth- ods that are classified based on input level which are: con- ventional intensive (CI), conventional moderate (CM) and organic intensive (OI) (Noponen et al. 2012). Conventional cultivation produces some of the most heavily chemically treated foods in the world with the high accumulation of synthetic fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides (Pimentel et al. 2005). In Vietnam, CI is the most popu- lar cultivation method due to the high yields and economic benefits. However, this method will have significant nega- tive effects to the environment and human health (Noponen et al. 2012). The CI is a coffee cultivation method that uses considerable amounts of chemical fertilizers, insecticides, fungicides and herbicides. The CM method in this study is defined as conventional farm without pesticides. Therefore, its negative effects on the environment are much less than those of CI. However, there are no synthetic fertilizers or chemicals, which are used in organic cultivation, thereby indicating high-quality coffee beans and great land, water and air quality where coffee is grown using organic manures, such as coffee pulp, animal manure or compost. Organic farms have moderate effects to the environment given their low-carbon emission. Thus, organic coffee beans are rich in antioxidants and compounds that benefit human health. Young coffee trees usually take approximately 3 years before its first harvest. The color of the coffee beans turns from green to red, with the latter indicating that its ripeness. Farmers in Vietnam usually manually collect coffee instead of using machineries. Coffee beans are annually collected from October to December. The harvested coffee bean was transported to the barbecue to dry.

<list>Drying of beans 
Coffee cherries are transported to large surfaces for easy drying and for the removal of moisture. Then, the coffee cherries are exposed to sunlight and mixed every day for total drying. Plastic sheets were used to cover the coffee cherries during the night and rainy days. It took approxi- mately 8-10 days to dry the coffee beans for the next stage of processing. 
Milling of beans 
After the drying stage, the coffee beans were placed in hulling machinery. The machinery sieved and removed the skin, pebbles and other material from the coffee beans. 
Packaging and storage

16: The goal of this study was to estimate and compare the environmental impact and identify the hot spot of conven- tional and organic coffee cultivation through LCA methodol- ogy (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006). The results of this study will be beneficial for proposing solutions to address the problem of sustainably growing coffee. Data from all the stages of coffee cultivation were collected by interview- ing the farmers who run the conventional coffee farms and referred from guidance documents for organic coffee cultiva- tion in the same area.

The LCA was used for the calculation of the CF and com- parison of the environmental impact of coffee production with different input levels (CI, CM and OI) from seedling to farm gate. The system boundaries of three coffee cultivation methods are shown in "Appendix" (Figs. 9, 10 and 11). The FU of this study is defined as 1 kg of green Robusta coffee bean in Tan Ha Commune, Lam Ha District of Lam Dong Province, Vietnam. All the input-output flows were normal- ized to the defined FU.

The data were calculated on the basis of the survey results of the interviews of 42 farmers. The study was conducted on farms with an area of approximately 2 ha or more. The inventory data were collected during 2017-2018.

SimaPro is the most extensively and foremost LCA software used in many countries for environmental impact assessment. SimaPro v8.3 was used in this study as an analy- sis tool for LCIA. The IPCC 2013 V 1.03 (for 100 years) and ReCiPe v1.13 were used for the assessment and comparison of the environmental effects of coffee cultivation for both midpoint and endpoint impacts for all 30 years of coffee cultivation. Specifically, IPCC method was used for analyz- ing the environmental hot spot of three cultivation methods to investigate key factors for environment impact. ReCiPe is the most current and consistent indicator available in LCIA which was used to assess the midpoint and endpoint impact (Huijbregts et al. 2017). The "midpoint impact" categories in this study are: Water Depletion (WD), Fossil Depletion (FD), Metal Depletion (MD), Natural Land Transformation (NLT), Urban Land Occupation (ULO), Agricultural Land Occupation (ALO), Terrestrial Ecotoxicity (TE), Particulate Matter Formation (PMF), Photochemical Oxidant Formation (POF), Human Toxicity (HT), Freshwater Eutrophication (FE), Terrestrial Acidification (TA), Ozone Depletion (OD) and Climate Change (CC). Two main categories CC and FE are of interest in Viet- nam and have attracted strong research attentions due to the significant negative impact to the environment (Azevedo et al. 2014; Vorösmarty et al. 2000). Besides, this study aims to provide a comprehensive and clear view of the effects of coffee cultivation on three important endpoint indicators (i.e., resources, human health and ecosystems). Furthermore (Table 1), two studies on coffee LCA (Noponen et al. 2012; Killian et al. 2013) were found to be similar to this current one. Therefore, the CF result in this research was compared with those of the two studies. Thus, we can compare the results of this study and other similar ones in order to assess the reliability of the study. However, the CF results in this study have been presented in an average year of the "full production" stage in order to compare the CF data from Noponen et al. (2012) and Killian et al. (2013).

20: The research scope is from seedling to farm gate and in a commune of Vietnam depending on the research objectives of the project. The assumptions and limitations of the study are as follows:

<list>Coffee is a perennial plant and has stable productivity for many years (Wellman 1961). 
Inorganic fertilizers are referred to as N, P and K, which are available in SimaPro and bypass the micronutrient content. 
Pesticides were calculated for the number of main ingre- dients, wherein the concentration is listed on the package. 
Machines and water pipe used in coffee cultivation were not considered because of the longevity of the coffee's lifespan. The organic farm was not applied widely in the study area. Thus, the inventory data were derived from a pilot farm and documents for organic coffee cultiva- tion and were provided by the Farmer's Association and agricultural engineers in the research area. 
The research did not consider evapotranspiration, rainfall and other climatic data (e.g., minimum and maximum temperatures, relative humidity, wind speed and sunshine hours). 
Co-products are coffee husks, which are used again as inputs to cultivation. Thus, the co-products were excluded in the study. 
The pruning process leaves some dried shoots, and the coffee leaves that decayed on-site over time were excluded in this study because of insufficient data.</list>

The research was conducted during 2017-2018 in Tan Ha commune, Lam Ha district, Lam Dong Province, Vietnam. A total of 42 questionnaires have been collected from per- sonal interview from 42 farms that have satisfactory quality of inventory analysis. Moreover, less variation is observed from the results of the interviews due to the same method of coffee cultivation. The interviewees are approximately 35-45 years old and experienced in agricultural cultivation with majority of them having more than 15 years in coffee farming. The average area of each coffee farm is from 2 to 3 ha with coffee trees more than 7 years of cultivation. This condition is the advantage for the survey, considering that most respondents are experienced people in large farming areas. This situation contributes to the reliability of the study when the sample criteria are met. The results of inventory data collection of the three cultivation methods (i.e., CI, CM and OI) are shown in "Appendix" (Tables 3, 4 and 5).

According to the growth stages of coffee, the data are expressed through four stages. The input data are divided into four parts (i.e., irrigation, fertilizer and pesticide appli- cation, machinery, and harvest and postharvest) on the basis of the farming process. Machinery is disregarded due to long lifespan. Coffee beans will be annually harvested from the third year onwards. Productivity will increase from the third year to the production initiation phase and then

gradually stabilize during the full production stage, after that productivity will decrease when coffee reaches its lifes- pan of 30 years in this study. The full production stage has the longest period with the highest input and yield. Cof- fee husk is combined with manure for composting. Organic waste including the branches, leaves from pruning and fallen leaves is naturally decomposed at the coffee farms. Ferti- lizer packages are reused and recycled. Generally, the input of the three farming methods is different. In particular, CI has the most input because this method requires coffee trees to be managed with large amount of water, fertilizer and pesticides. The OI has the least amount of inputs due to the limited use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides and preferred application of manure and manual pest control. In terms of growth stages, the full production stage is the longest period, and the input and output are high and stable. Production increased in the early years of initial production stage and decreased during the aging coffee period. How- ever, these results are based on the data collection. The data from LCI must be analyzed, evaluated and calculated by appropriate methods to obtain a deeper understanding. The specific results are presented in the following sections.

5: <header>Environmental hot spots </header>

As shown in Table 2, the hot spot of the CI farming method is the fertilizer and pesticide application stage. This stage accounts for 27.7 kg CO2e equivalent to 85.5% contribution to the environmental effect during the 30 years of lifespan. In particular, composting manure accounted for 39.7%, followed by phosphate fertilizer (P2O5) (24.4%), nitrogen fertilizer (13.2%) and potassium fertilizer (10.2%). It indicated that the level of effects of the environmental components can be affected by the comparison of the three cultivation methods. The results showed that CI contributed the high- est impact percentage, followed by CM and OI in all impact categories except WD. Moreover, CI accounts for the high- est percentage due to the large use of fertilizers, bovines and irrigation water as discussed in the previous section. The main categories that are affected by coffee cultivation are CC, HT, POF, PMF, ALO, NLT, MD and FD with per- centage higher than 50%. The use of organic and inorganic fertilizers led to FE and HT due to the accumulation of fer- tilizer residues in the water. The CC is an impact category with a percentage of the effects of the three methods larger than 75%. Evidently, the three farming methods greatly con- tribute to CC. In addition, the substantial water use during cultivation led to WD. The results show that the use of water in OI is greater than the other two methods. 2: <header>Endpoint impact results </header>

The results of the weighting analysis show that human health is mostly affected by all three farming methods than resources and ecosystem (Fig. 6). However, a difference in severity, decreasing from OI to CI, is observed. An analysis was conducted in this study to take a closer look at the fac- tors that can affect human health and their particular target areas. The result of endpoint impact for human health by ReCiPe is shown in Fig. 7.

Considering that a similarity exists between the three farming methods, we only analyzed only one case with the greatest effect here (Fig. 7). The results indicated that most of the substances affecting human health lie in air and water. The highest one is CO2 (37.67% over others) in the air, fol- lowed by di-nitrogen monoxide (11.30%) and sulfur dioxide (9.53%). The CO2 can be derived from the use of fossil fuels during the irrigation, harvest and postharvest periods.

4: <header>Carbon footprint results </header>

The CF results in this study showed that the CI method (0.920-0.949 kg CO2e) has the highest global warming potential (GWP) over CM (0.721-0.736 kg CO2e) and OI (0.640-0.647 kg COe) due to a higher amount of fertilizer application in CI than the two other cultivation methods. Moreover, the fluctuation in CF results of CI is the highest because this method has the most complex input (Fig. 8). This study was compared with three studies in Costa Rica and Nicaragua (Noponen et al. 2012; Killian et al. 2013). In Costa Rica, the CF values for 1 kg of fresh coffee cher- ries were 0.57, 0.46 and 0.35 kg CO2e for CI, CM and OI management systems, respectively (Noponen et al. 2012). In Nicaragua, the CF results for CI, CM and OI were 0.39, 0.25 and 0.32 kg CO2e, respectively (Noponen et al. 2012). In another paper, Killian et al. (2013) demonstrated that

the CF of the processes to produce 1 kg of green coffee is 1.02 kg CO2e in Costa Rica. Figure 8 shows that no CI dis- similarities are observed between this study and Killian et al. (2013). The studies in Costa Rica and Nicaragua had less CF assessment results than the other one because its scope only included fresh coffee rather than dry coffee at postharvest stage. Moreover, the variation in cultivation method, climate condition and input in different countries is likely to be the major factors affecting the CF results.

The collection of data and information was based on the results of the personal interview to ask about the knowl- edge, experience and diary of farmers on coffee cultivation (Bradburn et al. 2004). The LCI data included the use of non-renewable and renewable resources. Non-renewable

resources primarily consist of fossil fuels and minerals (Liang et al. 2018). However, agricultural machinery and irrigation tools are important components of coffee cultiva- tion in Vietnam, and the resource use and associated produc- tion were excluded from this study because of the longtime of usage. With regard to renewable resources, water usage was included because of its importance to agriculture in Vietnam. Lake water is mainly used for irrigation and culti- vation activities in the country. Some farmers use groundwa- ter and river water, but most of them have their own ponds in the area. The LCI data on diesel oil, gasoline, fertilizer and pesticides were also collected by personal interview via questionnaire.

Survey results showed that farmers usually buy cow and pig manure to compost manure fertilizers. Compost ferti- lizers are beneficial to farms, but the input amount should be controlled. Farmers stated that they often do not control the amount of inputs from compost that they will rely on farm finance. Farmers commented that they would supply their farms with as much fertilizer as possible. However, this input is limited by the farm finance and farmers still do not have the knowledge of how to calculate the amount of input appropriately. In addition, compost causes a high level of emission due to the excessive application that exceeds the limits that the environment can bear, which is consistent with Noponen et al. (2012). This finding is very important as the previous studies have demonstrated that composting manure has caused various environmental problems, such as nitrate leaching (Eriksen et al. 2004) and GHG emissions (Maeda et al. 2013). In general, the effect of fertilizers con- tributes to the environmental "hotspot" effect. According to Killian et al. (2013), fertilizers contribute to 95% of the environmental impact. Moreover, Noponen et al. (2012) noted that "the main CF emission hot spots for the conven- tional management treatments in both countries (Costa Rica and Nicaragua) were from fertilizer production." Therefore, training courses and knowledge sharing on how to appro- priately calculate fertilizer inputs for farmers are essential.

The excessive amount of inputs in coffee farms can cause many adverse effects on the environment, such as eutrophi- cation. The use of compost has also been found as a cause of eutrophication in midpoint impact result. This ingredi- ent exists in the environment because coffee plants cannot completely absorb all the phosphorus in fertilizers, which is consequently washed into groundwater and causes eutroph- ication (Werner et al. 2000). Besides, the results showed that coffee cultivation has had a big contribution to CC, but there is still no "deduction" of the ability to filter pollutants through photosynthesis, especially the ability to reduce CO2 in the air by coffee leaves. In addition, the level of plant nutrient absorption also depends heavily on soil types and natural conditions to provide suitable inputs. Therefore, regardless of the coffee farming methods, it is also important

to control the amount of fertilizers, and especially manure compost. Future studies on coffee LCA need to be clari- fied on this issue and should be clearly and efficiently com- municated to farmers. In addition, the results of the three cultivation methods are presented in comparative form for the purpose of comparing the severity of the three farming practices. Although it was shown that CI is the most nega- tive contribution to the environment (Noponen et al. 2012), many details have not been clarified. Here, the results dem- onstrated that inputs with large quantities and many types lead to CI as the highest CF coffee farming method. There are always different cultivation methods in different areas, countries and regions that need further research about the potential reduction in input of coffee cultivation and the increase in the quality of Vietnamese coffee.

In addition, compost causes a high level of emission due to the excessive application that exceeds the limits that the environment can bear, which is consistent with Noponen et al. (2012). This finding is very important as the previous studies have demonstrated that composting manure has caused various environmental problems, such as nitrate leaching (Eriksen et al. 2004) and GHG emissions (Maeda et al. 2013). In general, the effect of fertilizers con- tributes to the environmental "hotspot" effect. According to Killian et al. (2013), fertilizers contribute to 95% of the environmental impact. Moreover, Noponen et al. (2012) noted that "the main CF emission hot spots for the conven- tional management treatments in both countries (Costa Rica and Nicaragua) were from fertilizer production." Therefore, training courses and knowledge sharing on how to appro- priately calculate fertilizer inputs for farmers are essential.

The excessive amount of inputs in coffee farms can cause many adverse effects on the environment, such as eutrophi- cation. The use of compost has also been found as a cause of eutrophication in midpoint impact result. This ingredi- ent exists in the environment because coffee plants cannot completely absorb all the phosphorus in fertilizers, which is consequently washed into groundwater and causes eutroph- ication (Werner et al. 2000). Besides, the results showed that coffee cultivation has had a big contribution to CC, but there is still no "deduction" of the ability to filter pollutants through photosynthesis, especially the ability to reduce CO2 in the air by coffee leaves. In addition, the level of plant nutrient absorption also depends heavily on soil types and natural conditions to provide suitable inputs. Therefore, regardless of the coffee farming methods, it is also important

to control the amount of fertilizers, and especially manure compost. Future studies on coffee LCA need to be clari- fied on this issue and should be clearly and efficiently com- municated to farmers. In addition, the results of the three cultivation methods are presented in comparative form for the purpose of comparing the severity of the three farming practices. Although it was shown that CI is the most nega- tive contribution to the environment (Noponen et al. 2012), many details have not been clarified. Here, the results dem- onstrated that inputs with large quantities and many types lead to CI as the highest CF coffee farming method. There are always different cultivation methods in different areas, countries and regions that need further research about the potential reduction in input of coffee cultivation and the increase in the quality of Vietnamese coffee.

11: The results of this study have shown that CI has the highest impact on the environment, followed by CM and OI. The LCA results in hotspot assessment for fertilizer and pesti- cide application showed that the CI contributed 85.5% to GWP due to the high input amount of manure, while CM and OI contributed 80.4% and 68%, respectively, throughout the 30 years of cultivation. Moreover, fertilizers and pes- ticides greatly influence the human health, ecosystem and resources, contributing 75% effect in total. The CF result for one average productive year shows that the CI (0.935 kg CO2e) method has the highest GWP over CM (0.729 kg CO2e) and OI (0.644 kg CO2e) because CI has the highest amount of fertilizer application. Organic cultivation system has low environmental impact and CF results, which is thus better than CI or CM. The authors propose the application of the research and direction for further research on the basis of the results obtained and study limitations.

Many solutions are available to reduce the effects of cof- fee production on human health and environment, especially the influence of using inorganic fertilizers. Farmers should prioritize the use of organic compost from locally available sources. Moreover, farmers can develop local livestock sys- tems that use manure to decrease the distance of transpor- tation from other areas. Farmers should control the inputs when using manure or composting. The use of various ferti- lizers (organic and inorganic) is not beneficial to the coffee crops. Thus, farmers should adjust their inputs accordingly. Moreover, this study can be used by agronomists and agri- cultural engineers to train farmers. Finally, the results of the study reflect the current status of coffee cultivation in the study area, particularly its effects on human health and natu- ral resource. Therefore, the government should have a plan and orientation for the appropriate development of the area to protect human health and environment in the long run.

Future studies should expand the scope of research. Spe- cifically, the research area in Vietnam should be expanded to increase the scope of study from seedling to farm gate to cradle to factory gate or cradle to grave. In addition to the three methods of cultivation, several techniques, such as intercropping, automatic irrigation management and high efficient fertilizer application, should be included in combi- nation of the CI, CM and OI. These methods of farming are complex in terms of inputs, but few studies are available that evaluate and compare them with conventional techniques. The results of these studies are particularly useful in the future with respect to the balance of agricultural productivity with human health and environmental sustainability.
